hero.title: 7 Days in Bhutan.
hero.titleAccent: A Himalayan Cultural Escape with Punakha Valley.
hero.intro: The recommended package for first-time visitors who want more than a highlights tour. Seven days allows you to add Punakha — the old royal capital — to the Paro and Thimphu circuit, giving you the full breadth of Bhutan's most magnificent landscapes and most significant heritage sites, all without feeling rushed.
hero.heroImageAlt: Bhutan Punakha Dzong fortress at confluence of two rivers Himalayan landscape

includes: Meet & greet at Paro International Airport
Meals as per itinerary (B = Breakfast, D = Dinner)
All tours and sightseeing as per itinerary

overview.heading: 7 Days Bhutan Himalayan Cultural Escape: The Full Picture
body: Seven days is the package we recommend for visitors who want to truly understand Bhutan, not just see it. The additional time over the 6-day package is used for Punakha Valley — the former royal capital, home to the most spectacularly situated dzong in all of Bhutan, built at the confluence of two rivers and surrounded by terraced rice paddies that turn gold in autumn.

This itinerary covers Bhutan's three most essential valleys: Paro (Tiger's Nest, Rinpung Dzong, farmhouse experience), Thimphu (Buddha Dordenma, Tashichho Dzong, National Memorial Chorten, Wangditse trail), and Punakha (the Punakha Dzong, the Chimi Lhakhang fertility temple, and the Khamsum Yulley Namgyal Chorten across rice paddies). Together, these three valleys give you the complete picture of Bhutanese history, spirituality, and landscape.

The pace is deliberately relaxed. This is Bhutan — rushing defeats the purpose. Every site has the time it deserves, every day ends with dinner at the hotel rather than a race to the next destination, and the guide is your cultural interpreter, not just a logistics coordinator.

highlights.heading: The 7-Day Advantage — Why Punakha Changes Everything

items.title: Punakha Dzong — The Most Beautiful Dzong in Bhutan
items.body: At the confluence of the Pho Chhu (Father River) and Mo Chhu (Mother River), Punakha Dzong rises from the waters like a fortress from a medieval illuminated manuscript. Built in 1637 by Zhabdrung Ngawang Namgyal — the man who unified Bhutan — it served as the seat of government until 1955 and remains the winter residence of Bhutan's Je Khenpo (Chief Abbot). The interior courtyards, thousand-year-old jacaranda tree, and golden statue hall are among the most beautiful spaces in the Himalayan world.
items.imageAlt: Punakha Dzong Bhutan fortress river confluence Himalayan landscape

items.title: Chimi Lhakhang & the Rice Paddy Walk
items.body: A 30-minute walk across rice paddies (or golden terraces in autumn) leads to Chimi Lhakhang, the Temple of the Divine Madman — the revered and eccentric saint Drukpa Kunley, who used unconventional methods to subdue demons and spread Buddhism. The temple is a place of pilgrimage for couples seeking fertility blessings, and the walk to reach it — past farmhouses, children at play, and farmworkers in the fields — is one of the most genuine rural Bhutan experiences available.
items.imageAlt: Chimi Lhakhang fertility temple Punakha Bhutan rice paddy walk

items.title: Tiger's Nest — The Hike Without the Rush
items.body: With seven days, the Tiger's Nest hike falls in the final stretch of the journey, after you have absorbed the context that makes it meaningful. You understand by Day 6 something of Bhutanese Buddhism, dzong culture, and Himalayan spirituality — and when you finally stand in front of Taktsang Monastery on its cliff face, you understand why it has been a place of pilgrimage for 1,300 years, not just a good photograph.
items.imageAlt: Tiger's Nest Taktsang Monastery Bhutan Paro Valley cliff face sacred site

items.title: Extended Farmhouse & Cultural Immersion
items.body: Seven days allows for a longer, more relaxed farmhouse experience in Paro. Cook Ema Datshi. Try archery. Walk the kitchen garden. Have dinner with the family. This is the experience that separates a Bhutan tour from a Bhutan trip — a genuine human connection with how Bhutanese people live, far from the official monuments.
items.imageAlt: Bhutanese farmhouse Paro cultural immersion cooking archery extended stay

itinerary.heading: Your 7-Day Bhutan Himalayan Cultural Escape Itinerary

days.title: Arrival Paro — Journey to Thimphu
days.description: Arrive at Paro International Airport and meet your Dhesu guide. Drive to Thimphu via the scenic Paro-Thimphu highway, stopping at Tamchog Lhakhang and its famous iron chain bridge — one of the oldest suspension bridges in Bhutan, built by the legendary 14th-century engineer and saint Thangtong Gyalpo. Check in to your Thimphu hotel. Evening at leisure in the capital.

days.title: Thimphu Valley — Cultural Heart of the Kingdom
days.description: Full day in Thimphu. Buddha Dordenma (52-metre golden statue on Kuenselphodrang Hill), National Memorial Chorten (morning circumambulation with devotees), Motithang Takin Preserve, Tashichho Dzong. Afternoon at the Institute for Zorig Chusum to watch students learning Bhutan's 13 traditional arts.

days.title: Thimphu — Wangditse Trail & Heritage
days.description: Morning Wangditse Nature Trail hike through apple orchards and pine forest to Wangditse Monastery ridge. Afternoon Folk Heritage Museum (a furnished 200-year-old Bhutanese farmhouse) and Gagyel Lhundrup Weaving Centre for traditional textile demonstrations.

days.title: Thimphu to Punakha — The Valley of the Old Capital
days.description: Drive from Thimphu to Punakha via the Dochula Pass (3,100 metres) — on a clear day, the pass reveals a 180-degree panorama of the Eastern Himalaya with 108 memorial chortens in the foreground. Punakha Dzong in the afternoon — built at the confluence of two rivers, the fortress-monastery is the former royal capital and winter residence of the Je Khenpo. Khamsung Yulley Namgyal Chorten, reached via a 45-minute walk across rice paddies to a hilltop memorial stupa with views over the valley.

days.title: Punakha — Chimi Lhakhang & Return to Paro
days.description: Morning walk to Chimi Lhakhang (Temple of the Divine Madman) across rice paddies in Punakha Valley. The 30-minute walk passes through typical Bhutanese villages and farmland. Return to Paro in the afternoon via Simtokha Dzong. Visit Paro Rinpung Dzong and the Ta Dzong National Museum on arrival. Afternoon farmhouse cultural experience — archery, cooking Ema Datshi with a local family.
suggestedActivities: Rice paddy walk to Chimi Lhakhang

days.title: Taktsang Monastery Hike — Tiger's Nest
days.description: The legendary hike. Three hours up through pine and cedar forest to Taktsang (Tiger's Nest) — the cliff monastery 900 metres above Paro Valley that defines Bhutan for most visitors. By Day 6, you arrive here fully immersed in Bhutanese culture, understanding the spiritual context that makes this site extraordinary rather than just dramatic. After descending, a traditional Menchu hot stone bath.
suggestedActivities: Tiger's Nest Monastery hike (3 hours ascent)

days.title: Farewell and Depart Paro
days.description: Final morning in Paro for shopping — local thangka paintings, handwoven textiles (kishuthara and mathra), traditional masks, incense, and the famous Bhutanese postage stamps are popular purchases. Transfer to Paro International Airport.

hotelOptions.heading: Choose Your Comfort Level
hotelOptions.subheading: Thimphu and Paro hotels selected for authentic Bhutanese character and proximity to key sites. Punakha guesthouse or resort with river views.

tiers.label: Budget Friendly
tiers.hotel: Thimphu: Hotel Thimphu Central or similar | Punakha: Guesthouse or similar | Paro: River Front Resort or similar

tiers.label: Value Plus Comfort
tiers.hotel: Thimphu: Access Suite or similar | Punakha: Resort with valley views or similar | Paro: Tashi Namgay Resort or similar

tiers.label: Premium Stay
tiers.hotel: Thimphu: Hotel Yarkay or similar | Punakha: Luxury resort or similar | Paro: Rustic Roots Resort or similar

whyBook.heading: The Deeper Bhutan — For Travellers Who Want More Than the Highlights
whyBook.body: The 7-day Himalayan Cultural Escape was built for travellers who want to understand Bhutan rather than just visit it. Adding Punakha is not just an extra site — it is a different dimension of the country: the old capital, the most beautiful dzong, the rice paddy countryside that shows you Bhutan before the roads came. Dhesu has been running this circuit since 1988.

points.title: MATTA & IATA Registered
points.body: Fully licensed Bhutan tour operator. All visa applications, sustainable development fee guidance, and government permit documentation handled.

points.title: Punakha Expertise
points.body: We know when the Punakha Dzong is best visited (early morning), which rooms face the river confluence, and the best walking route to Chimi Lhakhang in different seasons.

points.title: Your Guide Is Your Cultural Interpreter
points.body: Our Bhutanese guides are chosen for cultural knowledge and English fluency, not just logistics. By Day 7, you will have learned more about Himalayan Buddhism than most people learn in a lifetime.

points.title: Recommended for First-Time Visitors
points.body: Of our four Bhutan packages, this is the one we recommend when someone asks 'what's the best way to experience Bhutan properly?' Seven days. Three valleys. Complete immersion.

sections.heading: 7 Day Bhutan Tour Package from Malaysia
sections.body:
A 7-day Bhutan tour from Malaysia is the recommended duration for first-time visitors who want a complete Bhutan experience. This Himalayan Cultural Escape covers Paro, Thimphu and Punakha — the three most significant valleys in western Bhutan — with enough time at each to do them properly.

The addition of Punakha is what separates the 7-day from shorter packages. Punakha Dzong, built in 1637 at the confluence of two rivers, is arguably the most beautiful building in the Himalayan world. The walk across rice paddies to Chimi Lhakhang gives you a Bhutanese countryside experience that no amount of Thimphu and Paro time can replicate. Seven days gives you Bhutan's full cultural depth.

sections.heading: Bhutan in 7 Days — What You Can Realistically See
sections.body:
In seven days of Bhutan travel from Malaysia, you can comfortably cover: all major Thimphu cultural sites (Buddha Dordenma, Tashichho Dzong, National Memorial Chorten, Wangditse trail), Punakha Dzong and the rice paddy countryside, Simtokha Dzong, Paro Rinpung Dzong and Ta Dzong Museum, a traditional farmhouse experience with archery and cooking, and the Tiger's Nest Monastery hike. This is the complete western Bhutan circuit.

The one thing you cannot do in seven days is reach eastern Bhutan (Bumthang Valley and beyond). For the eastern regions, a 10-14 day itinerary is required. Ask our Bhutan specialists about extended options.

items.question: Why is Punakha included in the 7-day but not the 5 or 6-day?
items.answer: Punakha requires at least two full travel days to do properly — one to get there (via Dochula Pass) and one to explore before returning toward Paro. Adding it to the 5 or 6-day itinerary would make the remaining days feel rushed. At 7 days, there's enough time to give Punakha the attention it deserves, including the rice paddy walk to Chimi Lhakhang, which is a highlight most visitors say they're glad they didn't miss.

items.question: What is the Dochula Pass experience?
items.answer: The Dochula Pass (3,100 metres) is on the road from Thimphu to Punakha. It is home to 108 memorial chortens (the Druk Wangyal Chortens) built by the Queen Mother and a small monastery. On a clear morning — particularly in autumn and spring — the pass offers a spectacular panorama of the Eastern Himalayan range, including peaks like Masagang, Tsendagang, Terigang, Jejekangphu and Gangkar Puensum (the world's highest unclimbed mountain). Even on cloudy days, the 108 chortens in the mist are atmospheric.

items.question: Is the 7-day package recommended for older travellers or those with limited mobility?
items.answer: The Tiger's Nest hike on Day 6 is the most physically demanding part of the itinerary. For older or less mobile travellers, horse riding is available for the first section of the ascent, and it is also possible to stop at the halfway viewpoint café which already offers spectacular views of the monastery. All other sites are accessible with standard walking. The rice paddy walk to Chimi Lhakhang (30 minutes each way on flat ground) is very manageable.

items.question: Can I add more days to this 7-day itinerary?
items.answer: Yes. The natural extension is our 8-day Bhutan + Nepal package, which adds Kathmandu for a complete Himalayan journey. Alternatively, we can extend the 7-day Bhutan circuit to add Bumthang Valley in central Bhutan, or add more days in Paro for additional hikes. Contact our Bhutan specialists for a customised itinerary.
